file_name: Paxton et al. - 2024 - Measuring AI Fairness in a Continuum Maintaining Nuances A Robustness Case Study.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and ResNet50, to perform skin lesion classification (binary task: benign vs. malignant/melanoma) on dermatology datasets (HAM10000 and Fitzpatrick17k). The study evaluated the fairness and robustness of these AI models against adversarial attacks in the context of skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, specifically a 'robustness case study' evaluating a new statistical method for measuring AI fairness in skin cancer diagnosis. It details specific experiments using machine learning models (CNN, ResNet50) on datasets (HAM10000, Fitzpatrick17k), applies adversarial attacks (FGSM, RN, SMP), and reports quantitative results and correlations, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Measuring AI Fairness in a Continuum Maintaining Nuances: A Robustness Case Study
brief_summary: This study evaluates AI fairness in skin lesion classification for melanoma diagnosis by treating skin color as a continuous multidimensional array rather than categorical groups. The authors employed Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and ResNet50 models on the HAM10000 and Fitzpatrick17k datasets to assess robustness against three adversarial attacks: random noise, Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM), and saliency map patch (SMP). The evaluation approach utilized statistical distance measures, specifically the Wasserstein distance, to quantify fairness nuances across the skin tone spectrum. Results indicated that ResNet50 generally maintained higher accuracy than CNN, but both models exhibited varying vulnerability to attacks depending on skin tone. The proposed continuum method revealed sharper correlations and hidden biases within skin color groups that were not evident in traditional categorical or scalar ITA approaches.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used in the HAM10000 dataset experiment as one of two selected classes (encompassing noncancerous conditions). Also referred to as 'benign' in the Fitzpatrick17k dataset experiment.
Used in the HAM10000 dataset experiment as one of two selected classes (one type of cancer). Also referred to as 'malignant' in the Fitzpatrick17k dataset experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories: benign (melanocytic nevi) vs. malignant (melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on skin lesion classification for the medical diagnosis of cancers, such as melanoma. The models (CNN and ResNet50) predict discrete classes (benign vs. malignant/melanoma) on the HAM10000 and Fitzpatrick17k datasets. The study evaluates classification accuracy, F1 scores, and adversarial attack success rates (False Positives and False Negatives) related to these diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine)
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive collection of 10,000 dermatoscopic images aimed at supporting research in the automated diagnosis of skin lesions, particularly melanoma. It was synthetically created with input from several hospitals and validated by medical professionals. The dataset exhibits a bias toward lighter skin tones. In this study, it was filtered to include only two classes due to the scarcity of dark-skinned samples.

use.training: Used for training the CNN and ResNet50 models (subset of 2000 samples).
use.validation: Used for validation of the CNN and ResNet50 models (subset of 1563 samples).
use.testing: Used for testing the CNN and ResNet50 models (subset of 1564 samples) and evaluating adversarial attack success rates.
use.other: Used to demonstrate the capabilities and limitations of the proposed fairness measurement approach using skin color gradation.

datasets_used.name: Fitzpatrick17k
datasets_used.description: A skin lesion image dataset with Fitzpatrick skin type labels assigned by dermatology experts, obtained from two dermatology atlases. It contains images with random lesion locations and often includes non-skin-colored areas. In this study, it was filtered to a binary classification task.

use.training: Used for training the CNN and ResNet50 models (subset of 2000 samples).
use.validation: Used for validation of the CNN and ResNet50 models (subset of 864 samples).
use.testing: Used for testing the CNN and ResNet50 models (subset of 864 samples) and evaluating adversarial attack success rates.
use.other: Used to demonstrate the capabilities and limitations of the proposed fairness measurement approach using skin color gradation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the statistical distance between the distribution of Individual Typology Angle (ITA) values of a sample image and a baseline image, thereby quantifying fairness nuances in skin color as a continuum.
To assess the relationship between skin color metrics (scalar ITA or skin color gradation) and Adversarial Attack Success Rates (AAML SR), including False Positive and False Negative success rates.

To convert skin color from CIELAB color space (lightness L and chromaticity b) into a continuous numerical value for each pixel, treating skin color as a multidimensional array distribution rather than a categorical group.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It utilized correlation coefficients and statistical distance measures (Wasserstein distance) to describe relationships and differences in fairness metrics across skin tone continua, but did not perform inferential statistical hypothesis testing.

HAM10000.notes: ResNet50 had better classification accuracy than CNN. For CNN, the F1 score dropped to around 66%. For ResNet50, the F1 score dropped to around 66% under attack conditions (text implies drop from baseline, but Table 1 shows 0.79 as test F1; text says 'dropped to around 66% (HAM10000)' for ResNet50 F1 score in context of attack vulnerability discussion, likely referring to specific attack scenarios not explicitly broken down in Table 1's F1 row, or a typo in the text vs table. However, Table 1 lists Test F1 Score as 0.79 for ResNet50. The text states 'ResNet50 had accuracy kept high performance, but the F1 score dropped to around 66% (HAM10000)...' which contradicts Table 1's 0.79. Given the instruction to preserve metric values exactly, Table 1 is the primary source for the final reported test metrics. The text highlights that SMP attacks showed a high SR of 80% in brown skin tones for the traditional method, and FGSM was the only one with successful attacks in all skin tone categories. The proposed skin gradation method showed a stronger correlation (-0.81) for FGSM FN with ResNet50 compared to scalar ITA (0.51).

Fitzpatrick17k.notes: Fitzpatrick17k results showed comparable AAML SRs for all skin color types in all attacks, albeit slightly higher for darker skin colors. FGSM SRs were higher for all skin tone types equally. No differences between models were seen in the traditional method. The proposed skin gradation method did not allow capturing the effect of skin color due to difficulty in extracting skin tones from the dataset images.

training_details.hyperparameters: Not explicitly reported; hyperparameters for adversarial examples were randomly decided and kept constant across models and datasets (detailed in Figure 1(b) of the paper).

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of skin images to CIELAB color space
Calculation of Individual Typology Angle (ITA) from average pigment (lightness L and chromaticity b)
Per-pixel ITA value calculation to treat skin color as a multidimensional array (skin color gradation)
Filtering of datasets to include only specific classes (benign/melanocytic nevi and malignant/melanoma) due to scarcity of dark-skinned samples
conclusions: Treating skin color as a continuous multidimensional array (skin color gradation) rather than categorical groups or scalar averages reveals nuances and hidden biases in AI fairness that are not evident in traditional approaches.

The proposed continuum method using statistical distance measures (Wasserstein distance) and correlation analysis on skin color gradation provides sharper correlations with adversarial attack success rates compared to scalar Individual Typology Angle (ITA) values.
ResNet50 generally maintained higher classification accuracy than the standard CNN model across both the HAM10000 and Fitzpatrick17k datasets.

Despite higher accuracy, ResNet50 exhibited a significant drop in F1 score (to around 66% for HAM10000 and 60% for Fitzpatrick17k) under adversarial conditions, indicating vulnerability to performance degradation.

Adversarial attacks, particularly Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) and Random Noise (RN), caused varying levels of misdiagnosis across different skin tones, with darker skin tones showing increased likelihood of false positives for RN attacks and specific vulnerabilities to FGSM.

The traditional categorical and scalar ITA methods failed to capture the effect of skin color on fairness in the Fitzpatrick17k dataset due to difficulties in extracting correct skin tones from images containing large non-skin backgrounds.

The proposed skin color gradation method successfully detected strong correlations between skin color features and attack success rates in the HAM10000 dataset, whereas the Fitzpatrick17k dataset remained unsuitable for this analysis due to image composition issues.
Adversarial Machine Learning (AAML) robustness is a critical dimension of AI fairness, as vulnerabilities to attacks can distort fairness metrics differently across sensitive attribute subgroups.
important_remarks: The HAM10000 dataset exhibits a significant bias toward lighter skin tones, with dark and brown skin types comprising only 0.7% and 0.9% of the data respectively, leading to the exclusion of other classes due to scarcity of dark-skinned samples.

The Fitzpatrick17k dataset contains images with random lesion locations and large non-skin-colored background areas, which prevented the extraction of accurate skin tones and rendered the proposed skin color gradation method ineffective for this dataset.

ResNet50 showed high test accuracy (0.88 on HAM10000, 0.80 on Fitzpatrick17k) but suffered a significant drop in F1 score (to ~66% and ~60% respectively) under adversarial conditions, indicating vulnerability to performance degradation despite accuracy metrics.

The study did not perform formal hypothesis testing; no p-values, significance tests, or confidence intervals were reported, relying instead on correlation coefficients and statistical distance measures.

Adversarial attacks caused varying levels of misdiagnosis across skin tones; specifically, Random Noise (RN) attacks led to higher false positive rates for darker skin tones, while Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) attacks showed strong correlations with false negatives for darker skin in the ResNet50 model.

The proposed skin color gradation method revealed sharper correlations with adversarial attack success rates than traditional scalar ITA or categorical methods, exposing hidden biases within skin color groups that were not evident in strictly categorical approaches.

Hyperparameters for adversarial examples were randomly decided and kept constant across models and datasets, which may limit the generalizability or reproducibility of the specific attack intensities used.
The study focuses on binary classification (benign vs. malignant/melanoma) and does not address fairness or robustness for other skin lesion types present in the original datasets.
